title: How to Do Parts Work in Daily Life
researcherTopic: radical acceptance, RAIN technique, Buddhist psychology
metaDescription: Learn how to apply the principles of Internal Family Systems (IFS) and parts work in your daily life to heal from complex trauma. Move from inner conflict to self-compassion.
imageDescription: A piece of shattered pottery is being carefully mended with gold, its cracks glowing with a warm, inner light. The hands holding it are gentle and focused, suggesting a process of patient, beautiful repair. The background is soft and out of focus, emphasizing the act of healing.
htmlContent:
The argument starts in the cereal aisle. One part of you is calculating the sugar content, another is remembering a childhood breakfast, and a third is screaming that none of this matters because there is a deadline to meet and you are, once again, wasting time. The body freezes, caught in the crossfire of a silent, internal committee meeting that has been in session for decades, a low-grade hum of competing needs that dictates the texture of your days.

The Parliament of the Self: An Introduction to Your Inner World

We have been taught to think of ourselves as a single, unified entity, a solo pilot at the controls of a coherent machine. Yet, for those of us who have navigated the disorienting terrain of complex trauma, this model feels not just inaccurate but significantly invalidating. The experience is often closer to being a reluctant chairperson of a loud, unruly parliament, where each member has a different agenda, a different history, and a different set of fears. This is the foundational insight of Internal Family Systems (IFS), a model that sees our inner world not as a monolith, but as a collection of 'parts'... These are not signs of being broken; they are evidence of a system that has done its absolute best to protect itself under impossible circumstances. I've sat with hundreds of people as they begin to map this inner territory, and the initial reaction is almost always one of relief... the realization that the inner chaos is not a personal failing but a coherent, if outdated, survival strategy.

Within this inner parliament, we can generally identify three types of members. First, there are the 'Exiles,' the young, vulnerable parts that hold the pain, shame, and fear from our earliest wounding. These are the parts that were overwhelmed by traumatic events and were after that locked away to protect the entire system from their intense emotional charge. Then, we have the 'Managers,' the proactive protectors that work tirelessly to keep the Exiles from being triggered. all dedicated to controlling our environment and our behavior to avoid any situation that might activate the pain of the Exiles. Finally, there are the 'Firefighters.' These are the reactive protectors that jump into action when an Exile, despite the Managers' best efforts, has been triggered. Their job is to douse the emotional fire at any cost, often through impulsive or numbing behaviors like substance use, binge-eating, or dissociative scrolling.

How to Listening Without Agenda

The first instinct when we encounter an internal part, especially a loud or critical one, is to argue with it, to reason it into submission, or to banish it entirely. But what if the goal was not to silence the part, but to understand its role? What if the inner critic is not a villain, but a terrified protector trying to keep you from repeating a past mistake? What follows from this is clear: the work begins to shift from internal conflict to internal diplomacy. The work of psychologist Tara Brach on radical acceptance offers a powerful framework here; her RAIN technique (Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture) provides a scaffold for this exact practice. We recognize the part is active, we allow it to be there without judgment, we investigate its sensations and beliefs with gentle curiosity, and we offer it the nurturing it likely never received. It is a striking shift from battling oneself to being with oneself.

This practice of listening is not passive. It is an active, engaged curiosity. It means noticing the Manager part that insists on checking email one last time before bed and, instead of obeying or fighting, gently asking it, "What are you afraid would happen if you didn't check?" It means feeling the wave of shame from an Exile and, rather than immediately distracting yourself, whispering internally, "I'm here with you. We are not trying to fix the part or solve its problem in that moment. We are simply offering it the one thing it has likely never had: our calm, non-judgmental presence.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link).

Translating the Body's Dialect

Bear with me on this one.

From Inner Critic to Concerned Guardian

Perhaps no part is more universally recognized or more deeply misunderstood than the inner critic. It is the voice that narrates our failures, anticipates our rejection, and compares us unfavorably to others. For years, we may have tried to silence this voice, only to find it growing louder and more insistent. Parts work invites a radical re-framing: this critic is not an enemy but a deeply misguided guardian, a protector part that adopted a harsh strategy in the belief that it could keep us safe. A client once described this realization as "like discovering the monster under my bed was just a scared kid in a Halloween costume." The work, then, is not to slay the critic but to enter into a relationship with it, to hear its fears, to acknowledge its protective intent, and to gently, consistently let it know that its services, in their current form, are no longer required. We can learn to say, "I hear your concern, and I appreciate you trying to protect me. I am an adult now, and I can handle this." What emerges is a long, slow conversation, a practice of building trust with the most wounded and defensive aspects of ourselves. You might find it helpful to explore other ways of reparenting your inner child to support this process.

The Daily Practice of Inner Diplomacy

Consider the simple act of making a decision. For many with a history of trauma, this can be an agonizing process, as different parts pull in opposite directions. A practical application of parts work is to externalize the conversation. Take a piece of paper and give each primary "voice" a space to be heard. Let the fearful Exile express its worries about failure. Let the cynical Firefighter part voice its skepticism. By getting all the perspectives out of your head and onto the page, you create the space needed for the Self to emerge and make a decision that honors all the parts, even if it doesn't please all of them. It turns out to be how we move from being paralyzed by internal conflict to being guided by internal wisdom.

A practical workbook for managing dissociation is Coping with Trauma-Related Dissociation (paid link).

The Emergence of Self-Energy

At the heart of the Internal Family Systems model is the concept of the Self... with a capital 'S'. The Self is not another part; it is the very essence of who we are. It is the calm, curious, compassionate, confident, courageous, creative, and connected core of our being. It is never damaged or destroyed, merely inaccessible. The entire goal of parts work is to differentiate the parts from the Self, allowing the Self to emerge and resume its natural role as the leader of the inner system. When we are "in Self," we are not blended with our anxious or angry parts; we are able to be with them, offering them the healing presence they need.

What Changes From Here

The ultimate goal of parts work is not to get rid of parts, for that would be a form of inner violence. The goal is integration ~ a state where the Self, our core of calm, compassionate, and curious energy, is back in the leadership role, and the parts can unburden themselves of the extreme roles they were forced into. An angry part can let go of its rage when it knows the Self can protect the system. A fearful part can relax when it trusts the Self to work through challenges. It is a return to an inherent wholeness that was never truly lost, only obscured by the smoke and debris of traumatic experience. Understanding the role of the polyvagal ladder can be an invaluable map for this process of returning to ourselves. It is the slow, steady work of reclaiming the seat of consciousness, not as a dictator, but as a wise and loving sovereign.

Here is the thing, though.

For practical self-compassion exercises, a popular workbook is The Self-Compassion Workbook (paid link).

What we find here is not the end of something. This is the beginning of a conversation between you and your own nervous system that most people never have the courage to start. The question is whether you will stay in the room when it gets hard.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is parts work the same as treating multiple personality disorder?

No, they are at its core different. Parts work, particularly within the IFS model, views these "parts" as natural aspects of a single personality, not as separate, dissociated identities. It is a non-pathologizing approach that sees all people as having an inner family of parts, whereas Dissociative Identity Disorder (formerly MPD) is a clinical diagnosis involving distinct and separate identity states that take control of the person's behavior.

Can I do parts work on my own?

While many people can begin to gently and safely explore their inner parts on their own using books, guided meditations, and journaling, it is highly recommended to work with a trained therapist, especially when dealing with significant trauma. A therapist can provide a stable presence and guidance when encountering heavily burdened or traumatized parts, ensuring the process remains safe and integrative rather than re-traumatizing. The key is to proceed with curiosity and gentleness, never forcing a part to reveal more than it is ready to.

A simple supplement that many find supports their mood is Garden of Life Vitamin D3 5000 IU (paid link).

What if I can't feel or identify any parts?

That becomes a very common experience, especially at the beginning. It may be that a protective part is active, one that is skeptical of the whole process or fears what might be uncovered. Instead of trying to force a connection, the work is to simply be with the experience of "not feeling anything." You can get curious about the sensation of numbness or blankness itself ~ that, too, is the voice of a part, likely a very powerful protector whose job is to keep overwhelming feelings at bay.
